description: Modern legal terms with accessible definitions, synonyms, and practical usage examples

terms.definition: The administration of justice in accordance with established rules and principles; the requirement that legal proceedings be conducted fairly and with procedural safeguards.
terms.practical_usage: Used in constitutional challenges to government actions affecting life, liberty, or property

terms.definition: A reasonable ground for belief in certain alleged facts; facts and circumstances sufficient to warrant a prudent person in believing that a crime has been or is being committed.
terms.practical_usage: Required standard for arrests, search warrants, and other law enforcement actions

terms.definition: A standard of proof less than probable cause but more than a hunch; a particularized and objective basis for suspecting a person of criminal activity.
terms.practical_usage: Justifies brief investigative stops (Terry stops) by police officers

terms.definition: The obligation to prove one's assertion; the duty of a party to substantiate an allegation or issue to avoid dismissal of that issue early in the case.
terms.practical_usage: In criminal cases, prosecution bears the burden; in civil cases, typically the plaintiff

terms.definition: The standard of proof in most civil cases; evidence that shows it is more likely than not that the fact sought to be proved is true.
terms.practical_usage: Civil litigation standard requiring proof greater than 50% likelihood

terms.definition: The standard of proof in criminal prosecutions; proof that leaves one firmly convinced of the defendant's guilt.
terms.practical_usage: Highest standard of proof, required for criminal convictions

terms.definition: An intermediate standard of proof between preponderance of evidence and beyond reasonable doubt; proof that produces in the mind of the trier of fact a firm belief or conviction.
terms.practical_usage: Used in civil cases involving fundamental rights or fraud allegations

terms.definition: The legal right to bring a lawsuit; requires showing of actual injury, causation, and redressability.
terms.practical_usage: Must be established before court will hear a case

terms.definition: The power and authority of a court to hear and decide a case; the geographic area or subject matter over which a court has authority.
terms.practical_usage: Fundamental requirement for valid court proceedings

terms.definition: The proper or a possible place for a lawsuit to be filed or a prosecution to be conducted; the geographic location of a court.
terms.practical_usage: Determines which court in a jurisdiction should hear a case

terms.definition: The pretrial process by which parties obtain information from each other to prepare for trial; includes depositions, interrogatories, and document requests.
terms.practical_usage: Crucial phase of civil litigation allowing parties to gather evidence

terms.definition: Out-of-court oral testimony given under oath and recorded for later use in court or for discovery purposes.
terms.practical_usage: Key discovery tool for obtaining witness testimony before trial

terms.definition: Written questions submitted by one party to another party in a lawsuit, which must be answered under oath.
terms.practical_usage: Discovery device limited in number by court rules

terms.definition: A formal request made to a court for an order or ruling; an application for a rule or order.
terms.practical_usage: Standard procedural mechanism for seeking court action

terms.definition: A judgment granted when there is no genuine issue of material fact and the moving party is entitled to judgment as a matter of law.
terms.practical_usage: Disposes of cases without need for full trial when facts are undisputed

terms.definition: A court order requiring a party to do or refrain from doing specific acts.
terms.practical_usage: Equitable remedy to prevent irreparable harm

terms.definition: A temporary injunction granted before trial to preserve the status quo until a final determination can be made.
terms.practical_usage: Granted when moving party shows likelihood of success and irreparable harm

terms.definition: Money claimed by, or ordered to be paid to, a person as compensation for loss or injury.
terms.practical_usage: Primary remedy in civil lawsuits

terms.definition: Damages awarded in addition to actual damages when the defendant acted with recklessness, malice, or deceit; intended to punish and deter.
terms.practical_usage: Awarded in cases of egregious conduct to punish wrongdoer

terms.definition: The failure to exercise the care that a reasonably prudent person would exercise in like circumstances.
terms.practical_usage: Foundation for most personal injury lawsuits

terms.definition: Liability that does not depend on actual negligence or intent to harm; imposed in certain circumstances such as product defects or ultrahazardous activities.
terms.practical_usage: Applied to inherently dangerous activities and defective products

terms.definition: Liability imposed on one person for the conduct of another based on the relationship between them, such as employer-employee.
terms.practical_usage: Holds employers responsible for employee actions within scope of employment

terms.definition: A duty to make good any loss, damage, or liability incurred by another; an agreement to reimburse for losses.
terms.practical_usage: Common in contracts to shift risk between parties

terms.definition: The substitution of one party for another whose debt the party pays, entitling the paying party to rights, remedies, or securities that would otherwise belong to the debtor.
terms.practical_usage: Allows insurers to recover from responsible third parties

terms.definition: A law that bars claims after a specified period; the time limit for bringing a lawsuit.
terms.practical_usage: Varies by jurisdiction and type of claim; begins when cause of action accrues

terms.definition: A matter judged; the principle that a final judgment on the merits bars further claims on the same cause of action.
terms.practical_usage: Prevents relitigation of decided cases between same parties

terms.definition: The binding effect of a judgment as to matters actually litigated and determined in a prior action; issue preclusion.
terms.practical_usage: Bars relitigation of specific issues already decided

terms.definition: A defendant's assertion of facts and arguments that, if true, will defeat the plaintiff's claim, even if all allegations in the complaint are true.
terms.practical_usage: Must be raised in answer or waived; includes self-defense, statute of limitations

terms.definition: A claim for relief asserted by a defendant against a plaintiff in the same action.
terms.practical_usage: Allows defendant to assert own claims against plaintiff

terms.definition: A lawsuit in which one or more members of a large group, or class, of individuals or entities sue on behalf of the entire class.
terms.practical_usage: Efficient resolution of claims affecting many similarly situated persons
